turn_result.narrative: The guard slides off his barstool and ducks beneath the low-hinged bartop, his boots thudding on the worn floorboards behind the bar. Spotting the iron ring set into the trapdoor, he heaves it open, releasing a breath of cool, yeasty air from below. With one hand trailing along the rough stone wall, he takes the cellar stairs one deliberate step at a time, disappearing into the amber dark beneath the tavern.

The guard sets down his tankard with a dull thunk and rises from his stool, scanning the smoky common room with a practiced eye. Satisfied that the patrons are behaving themselves, he rolls his shoulders and makes his way toward the heavy oak door at the front of the tavern. He pulls it open just enough to peer out into the cobblestone street beyond, one hand resting on the pommel of his sword.

The hooded figure moves with practiced, silent efficiency, a vial disappearing back into their cloak almost before the eye can follow. The patron raises his cup one last time, takes a long sip, and then a terrible shudder runs through him from crown to boot. He slumps forward heavily against the bar, his tankard rolling from limp fingers and clattering to the floor, drawing a few startled glances from nearby drinkers.

The guard sets down his tankard with a dull thud and straightens his tabard, scanning the warm common room one last time. Satisfied that the patrons are behaving themselves, he tucks his thumbs into his belt and walks with measured, unhurried steps toward the heavy oak door at the front entrance. His boots creak against the worn floorboards as he pushes the door open a crack, peering out into the lamplit street beyond.

The guard casts one last glance along the darkened street, his breath misting in the cool night air. Content that all is well beyond the tavern walls, he turns on his heel and pushes through the heavy oak door, stepping back into the warm, lantern-lit main hall.

The guard's hand falls to the pommel of his sword as his ears catch the telltale clink of glass echoing up from somewhere beneath his boots. He shoulders past the barkeep, ducks under the heavy oak door set into the floor behind the bar, and descends the creaking wooden steps into the cellar's musty darkness.

The guard ducks beneath the age-darkened lintel, one hand bracing against the rough stone wall as his boot finds the first slick step. The smell of oak, must, and cold earth rises to meet him as he descends into the dimness of the wine storage, his lantern casting long amber shadows across row upon row of dusty bottles.

The guard sets down his tankard with a dull thud and rolls his shoulders, scanning the common room one last time. Satisfied that the evening crowd poses no trouble, he marches purposefully toward the heavy oak doors at the front of the tavern, positioning himself where he can keep a watchful eye on the lamplit street beyond.

The guard squares his shoulders and pushes off from the entrance, boots thudding against the worn floorboards as he cuts a straight path toward the back of the tavern. He shoulders past a hanging curtain of beaded rope, bypassing the warm glow of the main hall entirely, and steps into the dim threshold of the back room.

The patron sets down his tankard with a dull thud and slides off his barstool, curiosity clearly getting the better of him. He ambles toward the doorway at the rear of the tavern, ducking slightly beneath the low-hanging beam as he passes through. His eyes sweep eagerly across the shelves lining the back room, fingers trailing along the dusty wood as he takes in the curious assortment of bottles, bundles, and forgotten oddities stacked there.

The patron's head snaps toward the sound of raised voices beyond the tavern walls. Seized by curiosity — or perhaps alarm — he shoves aside a stool and cuts directly through the back passage, bypassing the busy common room entirely. Within moments he is shouldering open the heavy door at the front of the building, stepping out into the night air toward the source of the noise.

The innkeeper wipes her hands on her apron and lifts the heavy iron ring set into the trapdoor behind the bar. She descends the creaking wooden steps into the cool darkness below, muttering figures under her breath as she begins tallying the barrels and crates of remaining stock.

The innkeeper's humming trails off as the bell's chime cuts through the quiet cellar air. He tucks the leather-bound ledger snugly under one arm, lifts the iron latch on the cellar door, and ducks through into the warmer light of the common room. His boots find their familiar path across the worn floorboards as he strides directly toward the entrance, a practiced smile already spreading across his broad face.

The guard rises from his stool with a grunt, tucking his thumbs into his belt. He shoulders past the heavy curtain that separates the common room from the back, his boots thudding softly on the worn floorboards as he disappears into the dimly lit storage room beyond.

The guard snatches his weathered cloak from its hook and tucks it under one arm, moving with practiced purpose toward a side passage. He navigates the narrow corridor that skirts the main hall entirely, his boots scuffing softly against the stone floor as he emerges directly at the tavern's front entrance.

The guard overturns a stool in his haste, vaulting over the bar top with surprising agility while the patron ducks beneath the counter right on his heels. Together they wrench open the heavy cellar hatch and tumble down the worn wooden steps, the door slamming shut above them with a thunderous bang that rattles the bottles on the shelves.

The guard sets down his tankard with a dull thud and rises from his stool by the hearth. With a practiced eye sweeping across the common room, he strides toward the heavy oak door at the front of the tavern. His gauntleted hand reaches out to test the iron latch, checking that the entrance is secure against the cold night beyond.

The guard's boots thunder across the flagstone floor as he charges from the entrance, shouldering past a startled barmaid and plunging through the cellar trapdoor in a single determined motion. Simultaneously, the hooded patron seizes the moment of commotion, ducking behind the bar with practiced ease and padding silently down the cellar steps in the guard's wake. Both figures disappear into the flickering darkness below, the trapdoor swinging on its hinges above them.

The guard's blade flashes in the firelight as he drives it clean through the patron's chest, a look of cold efficiency on his face. The patron crumples without so much as a cry, collapsing against the sawdust floor. Wasting no time, the guard seizes the body by the collar and drags it through the narrow doorway into the back room, the door swinging shut behind him with a dull thud.

The innkeeper wipes her hands on her apron and lifts the heavy iron ring set into the trapdoor behind the bar. With a grunt she heaves it open, releasing a breath of cool, musty air from below. She descends the creaking wooden steps one by one, a tallow candle held aloft to light the rows of barrels and crates waiting in the dark.

The guard tucks his thumbs into his belt and strides purposefully toward the back room, boots thudding against the worn wooden floor. He shoulders open the heavy door, eyes scanning the rows of stacked barrels with a practiced, suspicious gaze. Satisfied that something warrants a closer look, he steps fully inside to begin his inspection.

The patron pushes back his stool with a scrape against the worn floorboards, stretching his arms above his head with a low groan. He weaves lazily between the crowded tables, drawn toward the cool draft seeping beneath the tavern's heavy oak door.

The guard tilts his head at the familiar sound of barrels being shifted and footsteps scraping against stone below. Tucking his thumbs into his belt, he crosses the common room and finds the narrow cellar door, pulling it open to reveal a steep wooden staircase descending into flickering lamplight. He takes each step carefully, the old wood groaning under his boots as the smell of damp earth and aged oak rises up to meet him.

The guard, emboldened by a misplaced sense of authority, ducks behind the worn oak bar and plants his boot on the first cellar stair. He descends into the cool darkness below, the smell of old barrels and damp stone rising to meet him.
